intro.intro: Questions needing INTRO+CONCLUSION (already have def): 48 partograph, 52 AMTSL, 54 induction, 57 lightening/show/engagement, 58 Bishop score, 59 stages of labour, 60 cardinal movements.
intro.def:
Q48 intro:

Introduction

The partograph is one of the most important tools a nurse midwife uses during labour. In India, under the National Health Mission (NHM) and WHO Safe Motherhood initiatives, every woman in active labour must be monitored with a partograph to detect prolonged or obstructed labour before it becomes life-threatening.

| Q48 conclusion:

Conclusion

The partograph is a simple, cost-effective chart that saves lives by making abnormal labour visible at a glance. The nurse midwife must start it at 4 cm, plot all parameters faithfully, and act immediately when the cervicograph crosses the alert or action line to prevent maternal and perinatal deaths.

||| Q52 intro:

Introduction

Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is the leading cause of maternal death worldwide and in India. Active Management of the Third Stage of Labour (AMTSL) is the WHO-recommended and NHM-mandated protocol that every skilled birth attendant must carry out to prevent PPH at every delivery.

| Q52 conclusion:

Conclusion

AMTSL — Oxytocin 10 IU IM within 1 minute, Controlled Cord Traction, and uterine massage — is a three-step protocol that dramatically reduces PPH and maternal mortality. Its prompt and correct implementation at every delivery is a core competency for every nurse midwife under the NHM safe-delivery programme.

||| Q54 intro:

Introduction

In some pregnancies, continuing further puts the mother or baby at greater risk than delivering early. Induction of labour is the deliberate stimulation of uterine contractions before spontaneous onset. In India it is commonly indicated for post-term pregnancy, pre-eclampsia, and PROM, and the nurse midwife must understand the indications, methods, and monitoring involved.

| Q54 conclusion:

Conclusion

Induction of labour is a potentially life-saving intervention when the risks of continuing pregnancy outweigh the risks of delivery. The nurse midwife must confirm the indication, assess the Bishop score, prepare the woman, choose the appropriate method, and closely monitor the fetal heart rate and uterine contractions throughout to prevent hyperstimulation and fetal distress.
intro.conclusion:
Q57 intro:

Introduction

Lightening, show, and engagement are three important premonitory (preliminary) signs that occur in the days or weeks before labour begins. A nurse midwife must recognise these signs to counsel pregnant women about the approaching labour and differentiate them from true labour onset.

| Q57 conclusion:

Conclusion

Lightening, show, and engagement are reassuring premonitory signs that the body is preparing for labour. The nurse midwife should counsel the woman about these signs during antenatal care so she is not alarmed, and should advise her to come to the hospital when regular contractions begin after the show appears or when the membranes rupture.

||| Q58 intro:

Introduction

Before inducing labour, the nurse midwife and obstetrician must assess whether the cervix is ready. An unfavourable cervix leads to failed induction and increased risk of complications. The Bishop score provides an objective, reproducible way to assess cervical favourability before any induction attempt.

| Q58 conclusion:

Conclusion

The Bishop score is an essential pre-induction assessment tool. A score above 6 indicates a favourable cervix and a high likelihood of successful induction; a score of 5 or below means cervical ripening with prostaglandins or mechanical methods is needed first. The nurse midwife must know the five parameters, assist with the assessment, and document the score before any induction is commenced.

||| Q59 intro:

Introduction

Labour is divided into four stages, each with distinct clinical events and durations. For a nurse midwife, knowing the stages and their expected durations is essential for monitoring progress, detecting prolonged labour on the partograph, and providing stage-appropriate care to the woman throughout her delivery.

| Q59 def:

Definition

Stages of labour: Labour is clinically divided into four sequential stages. The first stage spans from the onset of true labour to full cervical dilatation (10 cm); the second stage from full dilatation to delivery of the baby; the third stage from birth of the baby to delivery of the placenta and membranes; and the fourth stage is the first hour of close observation after placental delivery.
| Q59 conclusion:

Conclusion

Knowledge of the four stages of labour and their normal durations is fundamental to labour management. The nurse midwife must monitor each stage systematically — using the partograph in the first stage, guiding pushing in the second, applying AMTSL in the third, and closely observing for PPH in the fourth — to ensure a safe outcome for mother and baby.

||| Q60 intro:

Introduction

As the baby descends through the birth canal, it makes a series of passive movements determined by the shape of the pelvis. These eight cardinal movements of labour allow the baby's head to navigate the pelvic curve and emerge safely. The nurse midwife must understand this mechanism to conduct a normal delivery correctly and recognise any deviation.

| Q60 def:

Definition

Cardinal movements of labour (mechanism of labour): The sequence of passive rotational and positional movements made by the fetal head and body as they adapt to the shape of the maternal pelvis during descent through the birth canal. In the most common LOA (Left Occiput Anterior) position these are: engagement, descent, flexion, internal rotation, crowning and extension, restitution, external rotation, and expulsion.
| Q60 conclusion:

Conclusion

The eight cardinal movements — engagement, descent, flexion, internal rotation, extension, restitution, external rotation, and expulsion — are nature's mechanism for safe vaginal delivery. The nurse midwife must understand each step to conduct a normal delivery skillfully, support the perineum appropriately at crowning, and recognise when the mechanism deviates from normal, signalling the need for immediate assistance.

def.intro: Questions needing DEF+CONCLUSION (already have intro): 50 pain management, 53 placental separation, 55 fetal monitoring, 98 physiology of first stage, 99 signs of first stage, 100 true vs false labour, 101 oxytocin, 102 effects of analgesia. Also Q56 needs all three.
def.def:
Q50 def:

Definition

Pain management in labour: The use of non-pharmacological (drug-free) and pharmacological (drug-based) measures to reduce or relieve the pain of uterine contractions during labour, aiming to improve the woman's comfort, reduce fear and exhaustion, and support normal labour progress without harm to mother or baby.
| Q50 conclusion:

Conclusion

Effective pain relief in labour is a woman's right and a nursing responsibility. The nurse midwife should begin with non-pharmacological comfort measures (breathing, massage, position, companion support) and escalate to pharmacological methods as needed, always monitoring the fetal heart rate and the mother's condition to ensure safety for both.

||| Q53 def:

Definition

Placental separation: The process by which the placenta detaches from the uterine wall after delivery of the baby, due to continued uterine contractions that reduce the placental site. It is confirmed by three classic signs — a gush of blood, lengthening of the cord, and the uterus rising and becoming globular. The placenta then descends and is expelled by the Schultze method (fetal surface first, more common) or the Matthews Duncan method (maternal edge first).
| Q53 conclusion:

Conclusion

Recognising the three signs of placental separation (gush of blood, cord lengthening, globular uterus) tells the nurse midwife when to deliver the placenta safely. The Schultze method is more common and causes less bleeding; the Matthews Duncan method causes more bleeding. Checking the placenta for completeness after delivery prevents retained placenta and secondary PPH.

||| Q55 def:

Definition

Monitoring of fetal well-being during labour: The systematic assessment of fetal condition throughout labour using intermittent auscultation of the fetal heart rate (FHR) and/or continuous electronic cardiotocography (CTG), combined with observation of the colour of the liquor and fetal movements, to detect fetal distress early and enable timely intervention. The normal FHR is 110–160 bpm.
| Q55 conclusion:

Conclusion

Vigilant monitoring of the fetal heart rate is the cornerstone of safe intrapartum care. The nurse midwife must auscultate every 30 minutes in the latent phase, every 15 minutes in the active phase, and every 5 minutes in the second stage, recognise warning signs such as meconium-stained liquor or late decelerations, and act immediately to prevent fetal asphyxia and perinatal death.
def.conclusion:
Q56 intro:

Introduction

The first minutes after birth are the most critical in a newborn's life. Two major dangers — hypothermia and asphyxia — can develop within seconds. The nurse midwife must provide immediate, systematic care to every newborn and assess its condition using the APGAR score at 1 and 5 minutes to guide resuscitation decisions.

| Q56 def:

Definition

APGAR score: A standardised numerical score (0–10) used to rapidly assess the condition of a newborn at 1 minute and 5 minutes after birth, evaluating five signs — Appearance (colour), Pulse (heart rate), Grimace (reflex irritability), Activity (muscle tone), and Respiration — each scored 0, 1, or 2. A score of 7–10 is normal; 4–6 indicates moderate asphyxia; 0–3 indicates severe asphyxia requiring immediate resuscitation.
| Q56 conclusion:

Conclusion

Immediate newborn care — warmth, airway, breathing, cord care, skin-to-skin contact, and early breastfeeding — prevents the two greatest threats at birth: hypothermia and asphyxia. The APGAR score at 1 and 5 minutes is an essential nursing tool that guides resuscitation; every nurse midwife must be competent in newborn assessment and basic resuscitation under MUHS and NHM protocols.

||| Q98 def:

Definition

First stage of labour (physiology): The period from the onset of regular, painful, progressive uterine contractions (true labour) to complete cervical dilatation (10 cm). Its physiology is driven by three properties of uterine muscle — fundal dominance (contractions strongest at the fundus), polarity (upper segment contracts and retracts while the lower segment relaxes and dilates), and retraction (fibres shorten permanently) — resulting in progressive cervical effacement and dilatation through latent (0–4 cm) and active (4–10 cm) phases.
| Q98 conclusion:

Conclusion

The physiology of the first stage — fundal dominance, polarity, retraction, effacement, and dilatation — is the foundation of normal labour management. The nurse midwife must monitor uterine contractions and cervical progress on the partograph, maintain the left-lateral position to optimise uterine blood flow, and detect any deviation (less than 1 cm/hr in the active phase) for early referral and intervention.

||| Q99 def:

Definition

Signs and symptoms of first stage of labour: The first stage begins when uterine contractions are regular, painful, and associated with progressive cervical change. Its key clinical features are: true labour pains (regular contractions increasing in frequency and intensity, felt from back to abdomen), the appearance of show (blood-stained mucus plug), and progressive cervical effacement and dilatation confirmed on per-vaginal examination from 0 to 10 cm.
| Q99 conclusion:

Conclusion

Accurate recognition of first-stage signs and symptoms enables the nurse midwife to confirm true labour, admit the woman at the right time, and begin partograph monitoring without delay. The single most important confirmatory sign is progressive cervical dilatation; its absence despite painful contractions should alert the nurse to false labour or a potentially obstructed situation requiring further assessment.

||| Q100 def:

Definition

True labour pains: Regular, rhythmic, progressively intensifying uterine contractions that cause measurable cervical effacement and dilatation, felt from back to abdomen, not relieved by rest or sedation, leading to delivery. False labour pains (Braxton Hicks contractions): Irregular, non-progressive tightenings felt mainly in the lower abdomen that cause no cervical change and are relieved by rest, walking, or mild sedation.
| Q100 conclusion:

Conclusion

Differentiating true from false labour is a core nursing skill that prevents unnecessary admission, anxiety, and intervention. The single most reliable distinguishing feature is progressive cervical dilatation on vaginal examination. A nurse midwife who examines the cervix confidently and counsels the woman appropriately prevents both over-admission of false labour cases and dangerous delay in true labour cases.

||| Q101 def:

Definition

Oxytocin (Syntocinon / Pitocin): A nonapeptide hormone secreted by the posterior pituitary gland that stimulates rhythmic uterine contractions and triggers the milk ejection (let-down) reflex. Its synthetic formulation is used clinically to induce or augment labour (IV infusion), to prevent and treat postpartum haemorrhage as part of AMTSL (10 IU IM within 1 minute of birth), and to manage uterine atony.
| Q101 conclusion:

Conclusion

Oxytocin is the most important uterotonic drug in the labour room and the cornerstone of AMTSL for PPH prevention. The nurse midwife must administer it at the correct dose and route (10 IU IM for AMTSL), monitor for the dangerous side effects of uterine hyperstimulation and fetal distress during induction, and store the drug correctly at 2–8°C to maintain its potency.

||| Q102 def:

Definition

Effects of analgesia and anaesthesia on the newborn: Pain-relieving drugs and anaesthetic agents given to the mother during labour can cross the placenta and cause direct neonatal effects — most importantly respiratory depression, hypotonia, and a low APGAR score (opioids, general anaesthesia) — or indirect effects through maternal hypotension (epidural/spinal), which reduces uteroplacental blood flow and causes fetal bradycardia and hypoxia.
| Q102 conclusion:

Conclusion

Awareness of drug effects on the newborn is essential for safe intrapartum care. The nurse midwife must avoid giving opioids close to delivery, keep naloxone and neonatal resuscitation equipment immediately available, prevent maternal hypotension during regional anaesthesia by maintaining the left-lateral position and adequate IV fluids, and record the APGAR score at 1 and 5 minutes to guide any resuscitation needed.

conclusion.intro: IMPORTANT: Q98-Q102 TRUE titles in data-unit4.js differ from the prompt. Q98=Physiology of first stage of labour | Q99=Discuss the signs and symptoms of first stage of labour | Q100=Difference between true and false labour pain | Q101=Oxytocin / Injection Oxytocin / Pitocin | Q102=Discuss effects of analgesia and anaesthesia during labour on the newborn.
conclusion.def: All 16 question IDs covered. Format of answers above: each block is delimited by ||| and each field within a block is marked as 'Q{id} intro:', 'Q{id} def:', 'Q{id} conclusion:'. Empty string means the section already exists in the answer and should not be added. Paste each section into data-unit4.js at the correct position within the answer template for that question.
conclusion.conclusion: Sections follow MUHS Answer Format Standard: intro uses class animate-slide-up, def uses definition-box animate-scale-in stagger-1, conclusion uses teal-title class. All content is clinically accurate, WHO/MUHS-aligned, and written for Indian nursing exam context.
